title: Decision Agents Bring AI into the Boardroom
gist: BCG argues the next competitive advantage in AI is 'decision agents'—a new agent class that operates at the point of strategic choice (not execution), assembling cross-functional evidence and testing scenarios live in the boardroom. Despite AI spend doubling to 1.7% of revenues, fewer than one-fifth of leaders prioritize decision making and only 14% of agent investment goes there, leaving the highest-value use case wide open.

insights.claim: Companies expect to double their AI investment in 2026 to 1.7% of revenues, per BCG's AI Radar 2026 survey of roughly 2,400 executives worldwide.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: A concrete, fast-rising AI budget baseline (as a share of revenue) that investors can use to size the enterprise-AI TAM and track spend acceleration.
insights.quote: companies expect to double their AI investment this year to 1.7% of revenues
insights.locator: AI Remains Under the Radar for Major Decisions

insights.claim: Most enterprise AI investment is aimed at operations, process, and task automation, and fewer than one-fifth of leaders rank decision making among their top AI investment priorities.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Signals an underinvested, high-value segment (decision support) where a vendor moving early could capture outsized share before the market crowds in.
insights.quote: Fewer than one-fifth of leaders ranked decision making among their top AI investment priorities.
insights.locator: AI Remains Under the Radar for Major Decisions

insights.claim: Around 42% of CEOs report personally using AI agents on a weekly basis, showing executive adoption is not the bottleneck to using AI for decisions.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: High personal CEO usage de-risks top-down adoption; the constraint is product/data, not executive willingness—watch for tooling that converts this habit into enterprise deployments.
insights.quote: some 42% of CEOs report they're now personally using agents weekly
insights.locator: AI Remains Under the Radar for Major Decisions

insights.claim: Even among companies that call decision making a top strategic AI application, actual investment in AI agents for it is the lowest of any topic at just 14%, exposing a stated-priority-versus-spending gap.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: A say-do gap this wide flags a whitespace market—the highest-consequence use case is the least funded, implying first-mover advantage for whoever productizes it.
insights.quote: the actual investment in AI agents is the lowest of any topic, at just 14%
insights.locator: AI Remains Under the Radar for Major Decisions

insights.claim: AI to date has improved how work gets done but not how critical decisions get made, leaving the most consequential, cross-functional, capital-deploying decisions largely untouched by AI.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Frames the next value frontier as decision quality rather than productivity—an investor lens for separating commoditized automation plays from durable strategic-decision platforms.
insights.quote: while it has improved how work gets done, it hasn't yet improved how critical decisions get made
insights.locator: AI Remains Under the Radar for Major Decisions

themes.theme: Decision agents as a new agent class

insights.claim: There are two basic AI agent types: execution agents that automate operational tasks with little human intervention, and decision agents that synthesize inputs, evaluate alternatives, and generate recommendations from explicit business logic.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: A clean taxonomy to categorize AI vendors—execution vs. decision—helping investors distinguish crowded automation from the emergent, higher-value decision layer.
insights.quote: There are essentially two basic types of AI agents.
insights.locator: What Are Decision Agents—and What Makes Them Valuable?

insights.claim: Decision agents are differentiated by operating at the point of choice rather than the point of execution, positioning them at the strategic decision-making layer of the enterprise.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: The 'point of choice' framing is the moat thesis—vendors that own the decision moment sit closer to capital allocation and are harder to displace than task automators.
insights.quote: they operate not at the point of execution but at the point of choice
insights.locator: What Are Decision Agents—and What Makes Them Valuable?

insights.claim: Decision agents create value in three ways: establishing a common evidence base across departments, developing and evaluating scenarios in real time, and limiting bias by grounding recommendations in shared data and explicit business logic.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: A three-part value framework to score any decision-support tool—products missing real-time scenario updates or bias reduction are weaker bets.
insights.locator: Decision agents can support individual managers or entire functional teams

insights.claim: Realizing full value from decision agents requires codifying individuals' tacit knowledge into machine-usable form alongside structured and unstructured data from emails and spreadsheets.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: The dependency on codifying tacit knowledge is a real adoption friction and a services/consulting revenue hook—watch which vendors solve knowledge capture, not just data plumbing.
insights.quote: individuals' knowledge—which needs to be codified in order for the agents to perform most effectively
insights.locator: They establish a common evidence base

insights.claim: By grounding outputs in shared data and explicit logic, decision agents reduce asymmetric preparation, unchallenged assumptions, and selective inputs, making tradeoffs transparent and recommendations traceable without eliminating subjective judgment.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Bias reduction and auditability are governance selling points that matter for board and regulatory acceptance—differentiators in enterprise procurement.
insights.quote: they make tradeoffs more transparent, recommendations more objective, and outcomes easier to trace
insights.locator: They limit bias

insights.claim: The most underappreciated yet most valuable use of decision agents is in executive committee meetings handling integrated business planning, portfolio investment allocation, and market-entry decisions.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Points investors at the specific high-stakes committee workflows where decision-agent demand will concentrate—capex allocation and market entry are the beachhead use cases.
insights.quote: Perhaps the most underappreciated, yet most valuable, use of decision agents is in meetings of executive committees.
insights.locator: Transforming Boardroom Decision Making

insights.claim: A custom decision agent can act as a boardroom team member—an 'omniscient chief of staff with supercomputing powers'—arriving with a preliminary memo-level assessment that it updates live as discussion unfolds.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: The 'AI board member' metaphor signals a product category (persistent, meeting-embedded agents) distinct from chatbots—watch for enterprise pricing tied to seat-in-the-room positioning.
insights.quote: an omniscient chief of staff with supercomputing powers
insights.locator: Transforming Boardroom Decision Making

insights.claim: In supply chain planning, key functions run on conflicting incentives—sales pushes volume growth, manufacturing optimizes utilization, and procurement seeks cost efficiency and supply continuity—with none having a clear view of the others' constraints.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Identifies the misaligned-incentive problem a digital-twin agent is sold to solve; the value prop is only as strong as its ability to reconcile these functional silos.
insights.quote: Sales, for instance, pushes volume growth, while manufacturing optimizes for utilization, and procurement seeks cost efficiency and supply continuity.
insights.locator: Supply Chain Management

insights.claim: A decision agent can act as a digital twin of the supply chain, mapping responses against supplier cost curves and recalculating scenarios immediately as leaders adjust volume assumptions and supplier choices mid-meeting.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Real-time recomputation against cost curves is the concrete capability buyers will test—vendors lacking live scenario recalculation are undifferentiated.
insights.quote: It then helps users assess the various scenarios and evaluate the tradeoffs, offering cost implications and feasibility. And all this analysis is performed on the spot
insights.locator: Supply Chain Management

insights.claim: Traditional executive-committee supply chain prep extends into weeks and still leaves leaders without an integrated view of options, whereas decision agents can save those weeks with a consistently maintained, codified data foundation.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: The 'save weeks' claim is the ROI hook for pilots—time-to-decision compression is the quantifiable benefit to underwrite investment.
insights.quote: Decision agents, then, allow leaders to save weeks
insights.locator: Supply Chain Management

insights.claim: For product innovation, a decision agent combines external competitive intelligence with internal feasibility and financial data to rank potential features, letting leaders adjust criteria weightings live while the agent updates the ranking.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Feature-prioritization is a second concrete workflow with clear inputs—useful for identifying which enterprise functions adopt decision agents first.
insights.locator: Product Innovation

insights.claim: For risk intelligence, a decision agent scores each risk against a common metric like contribution margin and calculates the breakeven point at which active mitigation costs less than the expected impact if the risk materializes.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Translating heterogeneous risks into a single financial metric and a mitigation breakeven is the killer feature for risk committees—a differentiator to look for in risk-AI vendors.
insights.quote: calculates the point at which active mitigation is worth the cost versus the cost of the impact if the risk materializes
insights.locator: Risk Intelligence Management

insights.claim: BCG advises starting decision-agent adoption with a pilot on one cross-functional, high-friction decision process such as integrated business planning, capex allocation, or new-market entry.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: A single-process pilot is the go-to-market wedge; investors can gauge traction by how many pilots convert to enterprise-wide rollouts.
insights.quote: Companies should choose one cross-functional, high-friction decision process to test, such as integrated business planning, capex allocation, or new-market entry.
insights.locator: Getting Started

insights.claim: Without a structured cross-functional data layer—a shared data lake or consolidation layer with a semantic layer holding codified business logic and ontology—even the most sophisticated agent logic yields unreliable results.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Data infrastructure is the binding constraint on decision-agent value—strengthens the investment case for the underlying data-layer and semantic-layer tooling that agents depend on.
insights.quote: Without this foundational layer, even the most sophisticated agent logic will yield unreliable results.
insights.locator: Ensure the right data infrastructure is in place

insights.claim: Decision agents support judgment but are no substitute for accountability, so companies must set governance assigning domain owners to each data set and business logic and defining data access and quality standards.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Governance and clear data ownership are adoption prerequisites—buyers without them stall, a signal for where implementation-services revenue accrues.
insights.quote: While agents support judgment, they are no substitute for accountability.
insights.locator: Establish a governance system

insights.claim: Companies should not wait for perfect data because imperfect data is still usable and AI's iterative ability means it improves over time.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: The 'don't wait for perfection' stance is a nudge to accelerate deployment—reduces the perceived barrier to entry and supports faster market adoption.
insights.quote: there is no reason to wait for perfection
insights.locator: Recognize the inherent constraints of data

insights.claim: Decision agents expose data and process gaps and reveal where accountability is unclear, so their adoption will challenge existing organizational processes and structures rather than slot in cleanly.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: Organizational disruption is a hidden adoption cost and change-management dependency—implementation timelines may run longer than the technology alone implies.
insights.quote: They also expose data and process gaps and reveal where accountability is unclear.
insights.locator: Expect adoption to challenge existing processes and structures

insights.claim: Decision agents should be treated as a structural, compounding investment—unlike other agents they create reusable foundations that make future generations of agents faster and cheaper.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: The compounding-foundation thesis implies increasing returns and lock-in for early adopters—an argument for durable advantage that investors can test against actual reuse.
insights.quote: that will also make future generations of agents faster and cheaper
insights.locator: Regard decision agents as a structural investment, not just another initiative

insights.claim: BCG X markets a productized 'Executive Decision Agent' and is deploying it with clients, indicating decision agents have moved from concept to a commercial offering with first-mover client adoption.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: A named commercial product plus 'first movers' claim signals the category is live and monetizing—track BCG X client wins as a leading indicator for the segment.
insights.quote: In working with clients to launch the Executive Decision Agent by BCG X
insights.locator: Getting Started

insights.claim: Closing the gap between AI investment and strategic decision making will be the source of the next competitive advantage, one BCG says will compound over time.
insights.speaker: Tobias Schmidt
insights.so_what: The core investment thesis of the piece—positions decision-quality AI, not productivity AI, as the durable edge; a lens for picking winners in enterprise AI.
insights.quote: Closing that gap will be the source of the next competitive advantage
insights.locator: How familiar is this?
